title: Measuring a Book with Paper Clips

steps.subgoalLabel: Line up the units end to end
steps.instruction: Place paper clips along the edge of the book. Start at one end.
steps.work: Place the first paper clip at the edge. Then place the next one right next to it, with no gaps or overlaps.
steps.explanation: For an accurate measurement, units must be placed end to end with no gaps and no overlaps.

steps.instruction: Keep placing paper clips until you reach the other end. Then count them.
steps.work: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. The book is 6 paper clips long.
steps.explanation: Count each paper clip that fits along the length. The total count is the measurement.

steps.instruction: Always say the number AND the unit you used.
steps.work: The book is 6 paper clips long.
steps.explanation: A measurement needs a number and a unit. Saying just '6' does not tell us much. '6 paper clips' is a complete measurement.

title: Why Different Units Give Different Numbers

steps.instruction: Measure a desk with large books.
steps.explanation: Books are big, so only a few are needed to span the desk.

steps.instruction: Now measure the same desk with crayons.
steps.explanation: Crayons are smaller than books, so we need more of them to cover the same length.

steps.instruction: Compare the two measurements. The desk did not change size!
steps.work: 3 books = 12 crayons (same desk). Smaller unit → bigger number.
steps.explanation: When you use smaller units, you get a bigger number. When you use bigger units, you get a smaller number. The object's actual length stays the same.
